questions.q: Which of Lenard's experimental observations of the photoelectric effect CANNOT be explained by the classical wave model of light?
options: Brighter light of the same colour ejects more electrons
There is a threshold frequency below which no electrons are emitted regardless of intensity
Light always travels at the speed of light
The metal plate becomes warm under illumination
questions.explanation: The wave model predicts that any colour should eventually free electrons if bright enough or given time, so the existence of a hard threshold frequency contradicts it. (The wave model actually does correctly predict that brighter light gives more electrons, so option A is not the failure.)

questions.q: Einstein's photoelectric equation is \( K_{\max} = hf - W \). What fundamental physical principle does this equation express?
options: Conservation of momentum in the collision
Conservation of energy applied to a single photon-electron collision
Conservation of charge
The wave nature of light
questions.explanation: The equation is conservation of energy for one collision: the photon's energy \(hf\) goes into paying the escape cost (work function \(W\)) plus the electron's leftover kinetic energy \(K_{\max}\). Energy in equals escape cost plus energy out.

questions.q: In the photon model, why does increasing the intensity (brightness) of light increase the NUMBER of photoelectrons but NOT their maximum kinetic energy?
options: Brighter light has a higher frequency, ejecting faster electrons
Intensity is the number of photons per second, so more photons cause more collisions; but each photon of a given colour carries the same energy \(hf\)
Brighter light heats the metal, lowering the work function
Intensity changes the work function of the metal
questions.explanation: Intensity equals photons per second. More photons of the same frequency means more one-photon-one-electron collisions, hence more electrons. But every photon of that colour carries the identical energy \(hf\), so each freed electron emerges with the same maximum kinetic energy.

questions.q: Why is there no measurable time delay between light striking the metal and electrons being emitted, even at low intensity?
options: Electrons store energy from many photons until they have enough to escape
The energy transfer is a single instantaneous collision: one photon hands all its energy to one electron at once
The metal pre-charges the electrons before the light arrives
Low-intensity light travels faster than bright light
questions.explanation: A photon delivers all its energy to a single electron in one collision, so the first suitable photon to arrive can free an electron immediately. There is no slow 'bucket filling' as the wave model required — emission is instant if \(hf \ge W\).

questions.q: Light of frequency \(7.0\times10^{14}\ \mathrm{Hz}\) strikes a metal with work function \(2.0\ \mathrm{eV}\). Using \(h = 4.14\times10^{-15}\ \mathrm{eV\,s}\), what is the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons?
options: \( 0.9\ \mathrm{eV} \)
\( 2.9\ \mathrm{eV} \)
\( 4.9\ \mathrm{eV} \)
\( 0.0\ \mathrm{eV} \) (no emission)
questions.explanation: Photon energy \(hf = 4.14\times10^{-15} \times 7.0\times10^{14} = 2.9\ \mathrm{eV}\). Then \(K_{\max} = hf - W = 2.9 - 2.0 = 0.9\ \mathrm{eV}\). Since \(hf > W\), emission does occur.

questions.q: What is the threshold frequency \(f_0\) for a metal with work function \(W\)?
questions.explanation: At the threshold the photon just barely frees an electron, so \(K_{\max} = 0\) and \(hf_0 = W\), giving \(f_0 = W/h\). Below this frequency no single photon carries enough energy to overcome the work function, so no emission occurs at any intensity.

questions.q: If you plot the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons against the frequency of incident light, what does the gradient (slope) of the straight line represent?
options: The work function of the metal
Planck's constant \(h\)
The threshold frequency
The speed of light
questions.explanation: Rearranging \(K_{\max} = hf - W\), the graph of \(K_{\max}\) versus \(f\) is a straight line of gradient \(h\) (Planck's constant), with x-intercept \(f_0\) and y-intercept \(-W\). The slope is the same for every metal — a signature of the photon model.

questions.q: Robert Millikan set out to DISPROVE Einstein's photon hypothesis. What did his decade of careful measurement actually achieve?
options: It proved the wave model was correct after all
It confirmed Einstein's equation precisely and yielded a value for Planck's constant matching black-body radiation
It showed the threshold frequency does not exist
It disproved conservation of energy
questions.explanation: Despite trying to demolish it, Millikan's data fell on the straight lines Einstein predicted, with the slope giving Planck's constant to within ~0.5% of Planck's value from black-body radiation. He inadvertently produced the most precise confirmation of the photoelectric equation.

questions.q: How does a solar cell (photovoltaic device) apply the physics of the photoelectric effect?
options: Heat from the sun boils water to spin a turbine
A photon with energy above the material's energy gap liberates an electron in the semiconductor, and the freed electrons are swept into a circuit to produce electricity
Sunlight is reflected to concentrate its intensity
The cell stores light as a continuous wave
questions.explanation: In a solar cell, a photon whose energy exceeds the semiconductor's energy gap frees an electron (just as a photon frees an electron from a metal), and these freed electrons are driven through an external circuit. This is Einstein's 1905 photon idea turned into hardware.

questions.q: Einstein's photon idea built on Planck's earlier work on black-body radiation. What was Planck's key (reluctantly proposed) assumption?
options: That light is purely a continuous wave
That atoms can only emit energy in discrete packets, each of size \(hf\) proportional to frequency
That energy is infinite at short wavelengths
That electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed paths
questions.explanation: Planck found he could only match black-body data by assuming hot atoms emit energy in discrete lumps of size \(hf\), introducing Planck's constant \(h\). He believed it was a mathematical trick; Einstein took the lumps to be real particles of light (photons), explaining the photoelectric effect.
